seo.title: MOFA Attestation in Pakistan: Process, Documents & Fees
seo.description: How to attest documents in Pakistan for use abroad: the notary, HEC, IBCC, NADRA, MOFA, and Apostille chain, with the correct order, fees, and timelines.

hero.h1: MOFA Attestation in Pakistan: Documents, Process, Fees and Timeline
hero.intro: MOFA attestation is the Ministry of Foreign Affairs stamp that makes a Pakistani document legally usable abroad, and it is almost never the first step. Before MOFA a degree needs HEC attestation, a matric or intermediate certificate needs IBCC attestation, and civil documents need NADRA or the issuing office. After MOFA, member countries need an Apostille and others need embassy attestation. This guide sets out the full chain in the correct order.

howTo.name: How to Get a Document Attested by MOFA in Pakistan

steps.name: Identify the document type and its prior authority
steps.text: Work out which authority must attest your document before MOFA: HEC for university degrees, IBCC for matric and intermediate certificates, NADRA or the issuing office for civil documents, and a notary public for affidavits and power of attorney.

steps.name: Complete the prior attestation
steps.text: Have the document attested by that prior authority first. MOFA confirms the earlier seal or signature, so a document with no prior attestation is turned away.

steps.name: Book a MOFA appointment
steps.text: Book an appointment through MOFA's online system and attend the Islamabad head office or a regional camp office such as Lahore with the original, photocopies, and your CNIC or passport.

steps.name: Submit and collect
steps.text: Pay the per-document government fee at the counter and collect the MOFA-attested document, which with an appointment is often ready the same day.

steps.name: Complete the final step for the destination country
steps.text: For a Hague Convention member country obtain an Apostille; for a non-member country complete embassy or high-commission attestation after MOFA.

bodyMarkdown:
**By Bilal Saeed, Advocate (Punjab Bar Council), Lahore High Court and District Courts Lahore. Reviewed and last updated 23 July 2026.**

This article is general information about Pakistani document-attestation procedure, not legal advice. Attestation rules, fees, and processing times are set by each authority and change periodically. Confirm the current requirements on the official [Ministry of Foreign Affairs](https://mofa.gov.pk) portal, or ask a lawyer, before you submit or travel.

MOFA attestation is the stamp from Pakistan's Ministry of Foreign Affairs that makes a Pakistani document legally usable in another country. It is almost never the first step. Before MOFA, a degree needs [HEC](https://www.hec.gov.pk) attestation, a matriculation or intermediate certificate needs [IBCC](https://www.ibcc.edu.pk) attestation, and a civil document (birth, marriage, death, or family registration certificate) needs verification from the issuing authority or [NADRA](https://www.nadra.gov.pk). After MOFA, a document going to a Hague Convention member country needs an Apostille, and a document going to a non-member country needs embassy or high-commission attestation. This guide sets out the full chain in the correct order, with the documents you need at each stage, an honest note on fees and timelines, and the reasons files get rejected.

## Key facts at a glance

| Item | Detail |
|---|---|
| Attesting authority | Ministry of Foreign Affairs (MOFA), Government of Pakistan |
| Purpose | Confirms an earlier signature or seal so the document is recognised abroad |
| Prior step (degrees) | HEC attestation |
| Prior step (matric or intermediate) | IBCC attestation |
| Prior step (civil documents) | NADRA or the issuing office (Union Council, District Office) |
| Prior step (affidavits, power of attorney) | Notary public or oath commissioner |
| Final step abroad (member countries) | Apostille |
| Final step abroad (non-member countries) | Embassy or high-commission attestation |
| Where | MOFA head office Islamabad and regional camp offices, including Lahore |

## Key takeaways

- MOFA is a middle step, not the first one. Get the correct prior attestation before you go to MOFA, or the document is turned away.
- Educational documents split by level: HEC attests university degrees, IBCC attests matric and intermediate certificates.
- Since 2023 Pakistan is part of the Hague Apostille Convention, so many countries now accept a single Apostille instead of full embassy legalisation.
- Fees are modest and set per document, but they change. Check the current schedule rather than relying on a fixed figure.
- If you are already abroad, a relative or lawyer can complete the chain for you using a properly drafted power of attorney.

## What MOFA attestation is and why you need it

A foreign university, employer, immigration authority, or court has no way to know whether a Pakistani certificate is genuine. Attestation solves this by building a chain of verification. Each authority confirms the seal or signature added by the authority before it. MOFA sits near the top of that chain: it confirms that the earlier attestation (from HEC, IBCC, NADRA, or a notary) is authentic, and it is the government's signal to a foreign authority that the document can be trusted.

You will typically need MOFA attestation when you apply for a study visa, a work visa or work permit, family reunification or spouse settlement, overseas employment through a licensed agency, or when you register a marriage, sale, or inheritance abroad. The exact set of documents depends on the destination country and the purpose, so read the checklist published by the relevant embassy or authority first, then attest only what is required.

## The attestation chain by document type

Different documents take different routes to MOFA. This table shows the prior step for the most common ones.

| Document | Prior attestation before MOFA | Final step abroad |
|---|---|---|
| University degree or transcript | HEC | Apostille or embassy attestation |
| Matric or intermediate certificate | IBCC | Apostille or embassy attestation |
| Birth, death, or marriage certificate | NADRA or the issuing Union Council or District Office | Apostille or embassy attestation |
| Family registration certificate (FRC) | NADRA | Apostille or embassy attestation |
| Affidavit or free-will affidavit | Notary public or oath commissioner | Apostille or embassy attestation |
| Power of attorney | Notary public, then relevant checks | Apostille or embassy attestation |
| Commercial or export document | Chamber of Commerce, where required | Apostille or embassy attestation |

## Step 1: get the correct prior attestation

### University degrees: HEC

Degrees, transcripts, and diplomas from a recognised Pakistani university are attested by the Higher Education Commission. HEC verifies the award directly with the issuing institution, so the degree must already be recorded with the university. Bring the original plus a photocopy, your CNIC, and the university-issued transcript. Verification adds time when HEC has to write back to the university, so start early if you are on a visa deadline.

### Matric and intermediate: IBCC

School and college certificates (Secondary School Certificate and Higher Secondary School Certificate) are not attested by HEC. They go to the Inter Boards Coordination Commission, which attests certificates issued by the education boards and also issues equivalence certificates where a foreign system needs a Pakistani grade converted. Mixing these up (taking a matric certificate to HEC, or a degree to IBCC) is one of the most common reasons a file stalls.

### Civil documents: NADRA and the issuing office

Birth, death, and marriage certificates, and the family registration certificate, are civil records. Where the destination authority asks for the computerised NADRA version, obtain that first. Older handwritten certificates often have to be reissued in the current NADRA format before MOFA will attest them. Marriage registration certificates come through the Union Council that recorded the nikah. If you are claiming an inheritance abroad, a related document you may need is the succession certificate: see our guide on the [NADRA succession certificate in Pakistan](/blog/nadra-succession-certificate-in-pakistan).

### Affidavits and power of attorney: notary first

Affidavits, free-will affidavits, and a power of attorney are drafted and then signed before a notary public or oath commissioner. The notary's signature is what MOFA later confirms. A power of attorney that authorises someone in Pakistan to sell property or operate a bank account on your behalf is a high-value document, and a defective one is a common route to fraud, so it should be drafted carefully. An [immigration lawyer in Lahore](/immigration-lawyer-in-lahore) prepares the affidavit or power of attorney, arranges notarisation, and then manages the MOFA step.

## Step 2: MOFA attestation

Once the prior attestation is on the document, it goes to MOFA. The main office is in Islamabad, and MOFA also runs regional camp offices, including one in Lahore, so most applicants do not have to travel to the capital.

1. Book an appointment through MOFA's online appointment system.
2. Attend with the original document, the prior attestation already completed, your CNIC or passport, and photocopies.
3. Pay the per-document government fee at the counter.
4. Collect the attested document, which with an appointment is often ready the same day.

If you cannot attend in person, a family member or lawyer can submit on your behalf with an authority letter and your identity documents. Keep clear photocopies of everything you submit.

## Step 3: Apostille or embassy attestation

This is the final step, and which route you take depends on the destination country.

**Apostille (member countries).** Pakistan joined the Hague Convention Abolishing the Requirement of Legalisation for Foreign Public Documents, and it has been in force for Pakistan since 2023. For destination countries that are members and that accept Pakistan's accession, a single Apostille certificate replaces the older, longer embassy-legalisation process. A small number of member states lodged objections to Pakistan's accession, so confirm with the destination country's authority or embassy that an Apostille will be accepted before you rely on it. Details of the Convention are published by the [Hague Conference on Private International Law](https://www.hcch.net/en/instruments/conventions/specialised-sections/apostille).

**Embassy attestation (non-member countries).** For countries outside the Convention, including several Gulf states, the document still needs attestation by that country's embassy or high commission after MOFA. Timelines and fees vary by mission, so check the embassy's own instructions.

## Fees and timeline: an honest note

Every authority in the chain (HEC, IBCC, NADRA, the notary, MOFA, and any embassy) sets its own fee, and those fees are revised from time to time. For that reason we do not quote fixed figures here. The government fees are generally modest per document. Treat any single number you read online as a starting point and confirm the current amount on the official portal before you budget.

Timelines are similar. With an appointment, the MOFA step itself is often quick, sometimes same day. The slow part is usually the prior verification: HEC writing to a university, or a civil record being reissued in NADRA format, which can add days to a couple of weeks. Build in a buffer if a visa or job start date depends on the attested document.

## Common reasons attestation is delayed or rejected

- Wrong prior authority: a degree taken to IBCC, or a matric certificate taken to HEC.
- Missing prior step: taking a document straight to MOFA with no HEC, IBCC, or NADRA attestation on it.
- Old-format civil documents that have to be reissued by NADRA before they can be attested.
- Name or date mismatches across documents (for example a certificate and a CNIC that do not agree), which are resolved with a corrective affidavit or a fresh record.
- Attesting the wrong route for the destination: seeking an Apostille for a non-member country, or embassy attestation where an Apostille was needed.

## Attestation while you are abroad

Overseas Pakistanis often need documents attested without being able to fly home. The chain can still be completed for you. A trusted relative or a lawyer, acting under a power of attorney, can run documents through HEC, IBCC, NADRA, and MOFA and courier the results to you. For property and banking matters handled this way, our [property lawyer in Lahore](/property-lawyer-in-lahore) page explains the overseas power-of-attorney process in detail. Where the documents support an asylum or protection claim, our guide on the [best countries for asylum](/blog/best-countries-for-asylum) explains how attested evidence fits into an overseas application.

## How a lawyer helps

Most attestation problems are procedural rather than legal, but the cost of getting the order wrong is real: a rejected visa, a delayed job, or a frozen property sale. A lawyer is useful when the document has to be drafted first (an affidavit or a power of attorney), when a name or record mismatch needs a corrective affidavit, when you are abroad and need someone to run the chain, or when an agent has quoted a fee that looks nothing like the official schedule. An [immigration lawyer in Lahore](/immigration-lawyer-in-lahore) at Saeed Law Firm prepares the underlying documents, arranges each attestation in the right order, and coordinates the final Apostille or embassy step. We do not guarantee visa outcomes, and we are transparent about which fees are government charges and which are our own. For a wider view of the practice areas we cover, see our overview of [lawyers in Lahore](/lawyers-in-lahore).

## Frequently asked questions

**What is MOFA attestation and why do I need it?**

MOFA attestation is the Ministry of Foreign Affairs confirming that an earlier seal or signature on your document is genuine, so a foreign authority will accept it. You need it whenever a Pakistani certificate has to be used abroad, for example for a study or work visa, family settlement, or overseas employment.

**What is the correct order to attest documents in Pakistan?**

First the prior authority (HEC for degrees, IBCC for matric or intermediate, NADRA or the issuing office for civil documents, a notary for affidavits and power of attorney), then MOFA, then either an Apostille for a Hague Convention member country or embassy attestation for a non-member country.

**Do I need HEC or IBCC attestation before MOFA?**

It depends on the certificate. University degrees and transcripts go to HEC. Matriculation and intermediate certificates go to IBCC. Taking a document to the wrong one is a common reason attestation is refused.

**Does Pakistan issue an Apostille?**

Yes. Pakistan has been part of the Hague Apostille Convention since 2023, so for many member countries a single Apostille replaces full embassy legalisation. A few member states objected to Pakistan's accession, so confirm the destination country accepts an Apostille before relying on it.

**How much does MOFA attestation cost?**

MOFA charges a modest per-document government fee, and the other authorities in the chain charge their own fees. All of these are revised periodically, so check the current official schedule rather than a fixed figure quoted online. Be cautious of agents charging far above the government rate.

**How long does MOFA attestation take?**

With an online appointment the MOFA step itself is often quick, sometimes the same day. The longer part is usually the prior verification, such as HEC confirming a degree with the university or a civil record being reissued by NADRA, which can add days to a couple of weeks.

**Can someone attest my documents while I am abroad?**

Yes. A relative or a lawyer acting under a properly drafted and notarised power of attorney can run your documents through the full chain and courier the attested copies to you.

**What is the difference between an Apostille and embassy attestation?**

An Apostille is a single certificate accepted by Hague Convention member countries. Embassy or high-commission attestation is the older, document-by-document process still required for countries outside the Convention. Which one you need is decided entirely by the destination country.

---

### Speak to an immigration lawyer in Lahore

If you need documents attested for a visa, job, or overseas matter and want the chain handled correctly the first time, Saeed Law Firm has practised in Lahore since 1975 and offers a free initial consultation. We draft the underlying affidavits and powers of attorney, arrange HEC, IBCC, NADRA, and MOFA attestation in the right order, and coordinate the final Apostille or embassy step. values: Ministry of Foreign Affairs (MOFA), Government of Pakistan

values: NADRA or issuing office (birth, marriage, death, FRC)
Notary public or oath commissioner (affidavits, power of attorney)

values: Hague Apostille Convention (in force for Pakistan since 2023)
Notarial and stamp law for affidavits and power of attorney
